reasoning: The CEO's $10M buy is the strongest insider signal in the entire portfolio — he has the most information and he's putting real money on the line at these prices. That said, the CyberArk integration drag is real and will suppress earnings for 2-3 quarters. At 85x P/E, there's no valuation safety net if execution stumbles. The position at ~5.5% weight and -16% P/L is appropriately sized — I won't sell into the CEO's conviction, but I won't add until CyberArk integration costs are absorbed and Q3/Q4 guidance normalizes. ENTRY/EXIT CONDITIONS: Add 5 shares on a break above $170 (50d MA reclaim) with volume AND Q3 earnings meeting/exceeding $0.78 guidance. Reduce if stock breaks below $139.57 (52W low) on volume.
entryConditions: Add 5 shares on a break above $170 (50d MA reclaim) with volume AND Q3 earnings meeting/exceeding $0.78 guidance

bullCase: Palo Alto Networks is the undisputed cybersecurity leader with NGS ARR at $6.3B growing 33% — the most important forward-looking metric. Q2 EPS beat $1.03 vs $0.76 (+35.5%). CEO Nikesh Arora bought 68,000 shares ($10M) on Mar 27, the day after the guidance miss — the most bullish insider signal possible. P/E 85x is at multi-year low vs historical 100x+. 67 Buy/21 Hold/0 Sell, avg target $210-216 (43% upside). Secular cybersecurity demand accelerating — Iran cyber threats escalating, regulatory mandates increasing. CyberArk integration is a short-term cost for long-term platform consolidation.
bearCase: Q3 guidance $0.78-0.80 well below $0.92 consensus — CyberArk integration costs will drag for multiple quarters, not one. P/E 85x still offers zero margin of safety. Stock crashed from $200 to $147 on the guidance miss. NGS ARR growth decelerating (33% vs 40%+ prior). RSI oversold at 37, below both MAs, 52-week low $139.57 as only support. Sector down 20% YTD, Fed hawkish punishing high-multiple names.
